mechanism: Given pairs of sequences with multiple outcomes occurring at different delays, the model weights the accumulated sum of all outcomes against a delay adjusted by outcome magnitudes. For each sequence pair, outcomes are summed linearly, delays are rescaled by a function of outcome magnitude, and the difference in weighted outcome versus weighted delay determines preference. The model predicts preference reversals and violations of independence through this nonlinear interaction between accumulation and delay adjustment.

Longitudinal panel data with repeated measurements over time are analyzed using a generalized estimating equations model to estimate marginal mean changes and group contrasts while accounting for within-subject correlation. Fixed effects for age and categorical interactions represent between-group differences in development trajectories. Marginal predictions are computed at specified ages to characterize developmental patterns.
domain: adolescent body image and weight concerns

mechanism: Experimental manipulation of a contextual cue (monetary symbols or words) is delivered to participants before attitude measurement. Differences in aggregate endorsement scores between treatment and control conditions are tested using between-subjects comparison, typically via t-tests or similar parametric tests on response scales. The strength of effect is quantified by group differences in mean responses.

Across multiple studies using different designs and measures, correlations and experimental manipulations test whether self-reported overconfidence (belief superiority) covaries with and predicts social status outcomes. Group-based observations of behavior are used to extract behavioral signatures; mediation and moderation analyses decompose the pathways linking predictors through intermediates to outcomes.

Observers encode and retain multiple feature dimensions (color, state, exemplar) of objects. A delay manipulation allows separate measurement of recall accuracy for each dimension and combinations thereof. Marginal recall probabilities and their joint probabilities are compared to test whether they decay together (bound representation) or independently (separate representation). Independence is tested via contingency analysis.

In a sequential dyadic exchange, one party makes a resource division decision (greedy, equal, or generous), then a second party observes this and makes their own division with a third party. Aggregate forwarding behavior (the division ratio chosen by the second party) is compared across treatment conditions. Mediation analysis tests whether negative affect explains the asymmetry between greedy and generous forwarding.
domain: reciprocity and cooperation in social exchange

mechanism: A learner observes outcomes sequentially and must infer an underlying rule or category. In active selection conditions, the learner chooses which observations to request; in passive reception, observations are provided. A sequential hypothesis-testing model predicts biases in sampling distribution that arise from the learner's current hypothesis. Performance (accuracy of inferred rule) is compared between conditions and against yoked controls.

Judges view video or audio recordings of deceivers and truth-tellers and make veracity judgments. Accuracy rates are compared across three conditions: immediate judgment, conscious deliberation, and unconscious processing (distraction period followed by judgment). Statistical tests compare accuracy against chance (50%) and across conditions. Integration of multiple information dimensions is posited to improve under unconscious processing.

Participants are exposed to or reminded of structure (patterns, order) in an unrelated domain, then subsequently measure their willingness to pursue a goal via self-report or behavioral choice. Differences in goal-pursuit intention or effort between structure-primed and control conditions are tested via between-subjects comparison. Effect magnitude is quantified by group mean differences.

Stimuli (nonsense words, names) are constructed so that articulation requires mouth movements directed either inward (front-to-rear) or outward (rear-to-front). Participants judge stimuli on preference or semantic scales. Preference ratings are compared between inward-articulation and outward-articulation conditions via between-subjects tests. In social conditions, frequency of choosing interaction partners with inward-name versus outward-name labels is tabulated.

Latent personality trait scores at two time points are compared within individuals using a latent change score model, which estimates unobserved change while accounting for measurement error. The effect of employment status at follow-up (still employed, unemployed, or reemployed) on the estimated latent change trajectories is tested via regression. Mean-level differences across groups quantify contextual effects on trait change.

In a reaction-time task, an observer views actions performed by two agents simultaneously and must respond by imitating or inhibiting these actions. Reaction time and error rates are recorded and compared across conditions: identical-action pairs (both agents perform the same movement), opposite-action pairs (agents perform opposite movements), and control conditions. Imitation effects are computed from differences in reaction time and error rates across conditions.

Participants make sequential prioritization decisions while pursuing multiple goals; outcomes of earlier decisions affect future available choices, forming a multistage decision tree. Expected value is computed via dynamic programming using specified utility functions. Observed prioritization patterns are compared to dynamic programming predictions using best-fit parameter estimation. Deviations are explained by risk attitudes (risk-aversion for approach goals, risk-seeking for avoidance goals) from prospect theory.

In a minimal group paradigm, perceivers view targets (with or without heuristic disease cues) and categorize them into in-group or out-group. Group membership bias and disease-cue effects on categorization are measured via proportion of out-group assignments. Individual differences in germ aversion and experimental priming manipulations modulate this bias. Between-condition and between-subjects comparisons quantify effects.

A requester makes an initial large request to a potential helper, who refuses. The requester then makes a smaller request. Compliance with the smaller request is measured and compared across conditions: smaller-request-only (control) and large-request-then-smaller-request (treatment). A reciprocity-of-concessions mechanism is proposed: the requester's concession (lowering the request) obligates the target to reciprocate. Compliance rates are compared via contingency tests.

Hypothetical scenarios are presented in which an agent performs an action with identical control and probability of success, but differing moral properties. Observers rate the intentionality of the action on a scale. The research argues that judgments reflect not the moral badness of the action itself but the moral blameworthiness of the agent. Mean differences in intentionality ratings between morally-negative and morally-neutral scenarios are analyzed.

Comparative statements are framed two ways ("more than" versus "less than" with equivalent logical content). Participants are presented statements and measured on preference, agreement, truth judgments, or frequency of use. Response proportions are compared across framings via within-subjects or between-subjects tests. A cognitive fluency manipulation (expectation of difficulty) moderates the effect. Mechanisms are evaluated via differences in processing fluency.

Participants rate attitudes toward organizations or hypothetical firms using Likert scales, with manipulation of firm label (for-profit versus non-profit) and priming conditions (long-run incentive thinking versus short-run or control). Mean attitude scores are compared across conditions via between-subjects tests and across experimental manipulations via ANOVA. Mediation analysis tests whether consideration of long-run profit incentives explains belief changes.

In negotiation scenarios, participants receive instructions about communication tactics (paltering, lying by omission, lying by commission, truth-telling) and then engage in a simulated negotiation task. Outcomes (claimed value, impasse rate, target perception of deception) are measured and compared across tactics. Mediation analysis tests whether targets' focus on deception impression versus speakers' focus on statement veracity explains the divergence.

Awe is elicited experimentally (vast imagery, nature exposure) or measured as a trait. Humility is measured via peer ratings, self-report scales, or behavioral indicators (acknowledging strengths/weaknesses, crediting external factors in success). Appraisals (vastness, worldview-challenge) and self-diminishment are measured as intermediates. Correlational analyses, mediation models, and experimental comparisons quantify the awe-appraisal-self-diminishment-humility pathway.
